literal: pertaining to mere appearance; from prati-bhāsa 'appearance against [something else]'
invariant_definition: The lowest level of the Advaita *sattā-traya* — apparent-only reality such as the snake superimposed on the rope, the silver superimposed on the shell-piece, the dream-objects of dreaming-state cognition. *Prātibhāsika* objects are sublated in subsequent *vyāvahārika* cognition (the rope is seen as rope; one wakes up). Distinguished from *vyāvahārika* (which is sublated only in *brahma-jñāna*) and from *pāramārthika* (unsublated on any level).

per_school.definition: *Prātibhāsika* objects are *anirvacanīya* but more locally so than *vyāvahārika* objects: they appear only to the individual erring perceiver and are sublated within ordinary waking-state experience. The category is one of the principal Advaita defenses against the charge that *māyā*-doctrine reduces all cognition to dream-status — *prātibhāsika* (dream / illusion) is firmly distinct from *vyāvahārika* (ordinary world).

translator_note: 'Apparent' or 'illusory' acceptable but the latter risks collapse with *mithyā* (which is the Advaita *vyāvahārika* status). Best: 'merely apparent' or *prātibhāsika* untranslated.
